topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the canvas, the vibrant depiction of a storm churning across the sea suddenly feeling… altered. A delicate, almost skeletal line, inked in deep indigo, snaked from the eye of the tempest down to the crashing waves. It wasn't part of her original vision. Her hand, usually steady with the practiced ease of years spent wielding a brush, trembled slightly as she leaned closer, tracing the foreign mark with a fingertip. Who had done this? She ran a hand through her already disheveled hair, the texture rough beneath her palm. A knot tightened in her stomach, a strange, unsettling feeling blooming behind her ribs. She stepped back, the cool air hitting her skin, and tried to re-examine the piece, searching for any clue, any misplaced element that explained the intrusion.

[story 2]

"This is… unexpected," Marcus murmured, his voice barely a breath as he circled the sculpture. He had spent weeks perfecting the textures of aged bronze, the subtle interplay of light and shadow meant to evoke melancholy. Yet, there, etched into the very surface of the figure’s shoulder, was a precise, geometric pattern, stark black against the warm patina. Marcus stopped breathing for a moment, his gaze locked on the anomaly. He took a slow, deliberate step back, his shoulders visibly tensing. He rubbed the back of his neck, a gesture of deep internal conflict. *Impossible,* he thought, the word tasting dry on his tongue. He needed to know the source of this addition, the hand that had dared to claim a piece of his creation.

[story 3]

I held the finished portrait up to the gallery lights, admiring the way the oil paint caught the illumination. Then, I noticed it. A series of fine, almost microscopic lines, woven into the shadow beneath the subject’s jawline, looked distinctly different from my own application. It was sharp, deliberate, undeniably an addition. My jaw went slack. I lowered the canvas slowly, my breath catching in my throat. My fingers instinctively curled into fists at my sides. I walked over to the security camera, my steps unnaturally quiet on the polished floor. The sheer audacity of it made my palms sweat. Someone had not just seen my work; they had marked it.
